prompt: How many players are on the court for one basketball team at a time?
In soccer, what does a red card mean for the player who receives it?
options: A penalty shot against their team
A free kick for the opponent

prompt: Which country invented modern lawn tennis in the 1870s?
In the Olympics, how many rings are on the flag — and what do they represent?
options: 5 rings, for the five continents that compete
3 rings, for mind, body, and spirit
6 rings, for the six inhabited continents

prompt: In cycling's Tour de France, the race leader wears a yellow jersey. What does the GREEN jersey mean?
options: Points leader — usually the best sprinter
The rider who won the most stages

prompt: In American football, a 'safety' scores points for the DEFENDING team. How many points is it worth?
Which sport uses a 'shuttlecock' — and why is it sometimes called that?
options: Cricket, because early balls were stuffed with cock feathers
Squash, because the rubber cock shuttles off the wall
Badminton, because the feathered projectile shuttles back and forth like a cock (rooster) pecking
Polo, because players shuttled between posts called cocks

prompt: What rare feat in baseball is it when a batter reaches base because the catcher drops strike three?
What is the maximum break (perfect score) in a single frame of snooker — and why is 147 so tricky to achieve?
options: 147 — pot all 15 reds each followed by a black, then all 6 colours in sequence
155 — only possible with a free ball awarded at the start
135 — you'd need to pot every red with a colour, then all colours in order

prompt: Ancient Greek Olympians competed completely nude. What Greek root word — meaning 'naked' — gives us the modern English words 'gym' and 'gymnasium'?
At the 1904 St. Louis Olympic marathon, Frederick Lorz crossed the finish line first and was nearly awarded the gold medal — until officials discovered the truth. What had he done?
options: He had swapped race numbers with a faster runner mid-race
He had started an hour early under a false identity
He had taken a stimulant injection supplied by his trainer
He had ridden roughly 11 miles of the course in an automobile

prompt: In cricket's Laws, a batsman could be dismissed 'Handled the Ball' — but this mode of dismissal was merged into another in 2017. Which one?
In golf, what is the term for a score of one stroke under par on a hole?
How many points is a touchdown worth in American football, before the extra point?
A regulation basketball hoop sits how high above the floor — the same height as the peach baskets James Naismith first nailed up in 1891?
How often are the Summer Olympic Games normally held?
In tennis, what is the term for a serve the opponent fails to touch at all?
Which sport features positions called pitcher, catcher, and shortstop?
In which sport would you perform a slam dunk?
How many players from one team are on the field at the start of a rugby union match?
A standard marathon is 26.2 miles. Why the awkward extra 0.2 of a mile?
options: The 1908 London course was lengthened so it would finish in front of the royal box
A surveyor's error in the 1896 Athens Games was never corrected
It rounds up an ancient Greek measurement that didn't convert cleanly
Organisers added a lap of the track to use up leftover tickets

prompt: In Formula 1, what does the black-and-white chequered flag signify?
options: The end of the race or session
The start of the formation lap

prompt: In the sport of sumo, how does a wrestler most commonly win a bout?
options: Lifting the opponent clean off the ground
Forcing the opponent out of the ring or making them touch the ground with anything but their feet
Pinning the opponent's shoulders to the mat for three seconds
Scoring the most points over three timed rounds

prompt: Which boxer famously declared 'I am the greatest' and promised to 'float like a butterfly, sting like a bee'?
A boxing 'ring' is actually square. Why is it still called a ring?
options: Fighters touched a ring on the referee's hand to begin
The first champion's surname was Ring
Early bare-knuckle fights were held inside a circle drawn on the ground, and the name stuck
The corner posts were originally shaped like giant rings

prompt: Which country has won the most FIFA Men's World Cup titles?
A '180' is the highest score with three darts in a single throw. Which bed must all three darts land in to reach it?
Most figure-skating jumps take off facing backward. Which jump is the exception — the only one entered facing forward, named after the skater who first landed it?
Why is the Stanley Cup, awarded to the NHL champions, unusual compared with most championship trophies?
options: Only the team captain is ever allowed to touch it
A brand-new one is forged from melted-down hockey skates each season
Each winning player's name is engraved on it, and the same physical cup is passed on year after year
It is made entirely of solid gold and never leaves the league's vault

prompt: An Olympic 'gold' medal is mostly made of which metal, with only about six grams of actual gold plating it?
The Fosbury Flop revolutionised one Olympic event by having athletes clear the bar headfirst and on their backs. Which event?
At Wimbledon, players must follow a famously strict dress rule that the other three tennis majors don't impose. What is it?
options: They must use only wooden-framed rackets
They must wear almost entirely white
They are forbidden from wearing any headwear
They may not display a sponsor's logo of any kind
